scenarioDescription: A financial institution has 10 million historical loan applications with 200 features stored in S3. Before training a credit risk model, they need to identify which features are most predictive, remove highly correlated features, and transform categorical variables using target encoding — all at scale without managing infrastructure.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler|Amazon SageMaker Processing|AWS Glue ETL|Amazon SageMaker Feature Store|Amazon Athena|Amazon EMR|Amazon SageMaker Autopilot
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler|Amazon SageMaker Processing|Amazon SageMaker Feature Store
hint: A visual data preparation tool lets you interactively explore feature importance, correlations, and apply 300+ built-in transformations without code.
hint2: After designing transformations visually, you can export them as Processing jobs for production-scale execution.
explanation: Data resides in Amazon S3. Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler provides a visual interface to explore feature correlations, compute feature importance rankings, and design transformation flows including target encoding — all interactively. The designed flow is exported to Amazon SageMaker Processing for scalable, production-grade execution across the full 10M dataset. Transformed features are stored in Amazon SageMaker Feature Store for consistent reuse across training and inference pipelines.

scenarioDescription: A healthcare company needs to train a medical image classification model on 500,000 chest X-rays. The images are 2048x2048 pixels, training requires multiple GPUs, and the team wants to use a pre-trained ResNet-50 model with transfer learning. They need to minimize training time while controlling costs.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Training|Amazon EC2 P4d|Amazon SageMaker Built-in Image Classification|AWS Lambda|Amazon Rekognition|Amazon SageMaker Debugger|Amazon SageMaker Spot Training
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker Training|Amazon SageMaker Debugger|Amazon SageMaker Spot Training
hint: SageMaker Training supports custom training scripts with PyTorch/TensorFlow and multi-GPU distributed training using Horovod or SageMaker Distributed.
hint2: Managed Spot Training can reduce training costs by up to 90% by using EC2 Spot Instances with automatic checkpointing.
explanation: Chest X-ray images are stored in Amazon S3. Amazon SageMaker Training runs the custom transfer learning job using a pre-trained ResNet-50 with multi-GPU instances (ml.p4d.24xlarge) and SageMaker's distributed training libraries for parallelism. Amazon SageMaker Debugger monitors training in real-time, detecting issues like vanishing gradients or overfitting. Amazon SageMaker Spot Training uses Spot Instances with automatic checkpointing to reduce costs by up to 90% while handling interruptions gracefully.

scenarioDescription: An e-commerce company wants to build a product recommendation engine. They have implicit feedback data (clicks, views, purchases) for 50 million users and 2 million products. They need a collaborative filtering approach that handles the sparse implicit feedback matrix and scales to this dataset size.
toolboxServices: Amazon Personalize|Amazon SageMaker Factorization Machines|Amazon SageMaker Built-in Algorithms|Amazon Neptune|Amazon SageMaker KNN|Amazon Comprehend|Amazon Kendra
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker Factorization Machines|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: SageMaker's built-in Factorization Machines algorithm is specifically designed for sparse, high-dimensional datasets like implicit feedback matrices.
hint2: Factorization Machines learn latent factor representations for users and products, handling the sparsity that standard matrix factorization struggles with.
explanation: Implicit feedback data is stored in Amazon S3 in recordIO-protobuf format for efficient streaming. Amazon SageMaker Factorization Machines trains a collaborative filtering model that decomposes the sparse user-product interaction matrix into low-rank latent factors, handling the extreme sparsity of implicit feedback (most user-product pairs have no interaction). The trained model is deployed to Amazon SageMaker Hosting for real-time recommendation inference at scale.

scenarioDescription: A manufacturing company has sensor data from 10,000 machines with 50 metrics each, sampled every second. They need to detect anomalous machine behavior in real-time without labeled anomaly data. The solution must identify which specific metrics are contributing to each detected anomaly.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Random Cut Forest|Amazon Kinesis Data Streams|Amazon SageMaker IP Insights|Amazon CloudWatch Anomaly Detection|AWS Lambda|Amazon Lookout for Equipment|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams|AWS Lambda|Amazon SageMaker Random Cut Forest|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: Random Cut Forest (RCF) is SageMaker's built-in unsupervised anomaly detection algorithm — it requires no labeled data and assigns anomaly scores.
hint2: RCF provides attribution scores that indicate which features contributed most to each anomaly detection, enabling root cause analysis.
explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams ingests the real-time sensor data from 10,000 machines. AWS Lambda preprocesses and formats the streaming data for inference. Amazon SageMaker Random Cut Forest is the ideal unsupervised algorithm — it detects anomalies without labeled data by learning the normal distribution of the multivariate sensor readings and assigning anomaly scores to deviations. The model is deployed on Amazon SageMaker Hosting for real-time inference, and RCF's built-in attribution scores identify which specific metrics are driving each anomaly.

scenarioDescription: A data science team has trained an XGBoost model for customer churn prediction. Before deploying to production, they need to understand which features drive individual predictions (not just global importance), detect potential bias across demographic groups, and generate model explanation reports for regulatory compliance.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Clarify|Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor|SHAP|Amazon SageMaker Debugger|AWS Lambda|Amazon SageMaker Experiments|Amazon Augmented AI
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon SageMaker Clarify|Amazon SageMaker Clarify|Amazon S3
hint: SageMaker Clarify uses SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values to compute feature attributions for individual predictions.
hint2: Clarify also computes bias metrics like Disparate Impact (DI) and Conditional Demographic Disparity (CDD) across protected attributes.
explanation: Amazon SageMaker Clarify first runs bias detection analysis, computing pre-training and post-training bias metrics (Disparate Impact, Demographic Parity Difference, etc.) across demographic groups to identify potential discrimination. Clarify then generates SHAP-based feature attribution reports that explain individual predictions — showing which features pushed each customer toward or away from churn — satisfying regulatory explainability requirements. All reports are saved to Amazon S3 for compliance documentation and audit trails.

scenarioDescription: A media company needs to automatically transcribe customer support calls in real-time, detect the sentiment of each speaker turn, identify key entities (product names, account numbers), and route negative-sentiment calls to supervisors immediately.
toolboxServices: Amazon Transcribe|Amazon Comprehend|Amazon Lex|Amazon Polly|Amazon Kinesis Data Streams|AWS Lambda|Amazon Connect
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Transcribe|Amazon Comprehend|AWS Lambda|Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
hint: A managed speech-to-text service supports real-time streaming transcription with speaker diarization.
hint2: After transcription, an NLP service can perform sentiment analysis and entity extraction on each speaker turn simultaneously.
explanation: Amazon Transcribe performs real-time streaming speech-to-text conversion with speaker diarization to separate each speaker's turns. Amazon Comprehend analyzes each transcribed turn for sentiment (positive, negative, neutral, mixed) and extracts named entities like product names and account numbers. AWS Lambda evaluates the sentiment scores and triggers routing logic — when negative sentiment is detected, it publishes an event to Amazon Kinesis Data Streams for real-time supervisor notification and escalation.

scenarioDescription: A data science team needs to train a text classification model on 5 million customer reviews. The reviews are raw text with no feature engineering. They need an algorithm that automatically learns word embeddings and text representations without manual feature extraction, and trains faster than traditional NLP approaches.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker BlazingText|Amazon Comprehend Custom Classification|Amazon SageMaker NTM|Amazon SageMaker Seq2Seq|Amazon Kendra|Amazon Textract|Amazon SageMaker Object2Vec
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker BlazingText|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: BlazingText in supervised mode is an optimized implementation of the fastText algorithm for text classification.
hint2: BlazingText automatically learns word n-gram representations and trains orders of magnitude faster than traditional deep learning NLP models.
explanation: Raw customer reviews are stored in Amazon S3 in BlazingText's expected format (label followed by text per line). Amazon SageMaker BlazingText in supervised mode trains a text classification model using the fastText algorithm — it automatically learns subword embeddings and word n-gram features without manual feature engineering, training on millions of examples in minutes rather than hours. The trained model is deployed to Amazon SageMaker Hosting for real-time classification of new reviews.

scenarioDescription: A company has deployed a fraud detection model to production. Over the past 3 months, they've noticed prediction accuracy degrading. They need to automatically detect when the input data distribution shifts from the training data, identify which features are drifting, and trigger model retraining when drift exceeds thresholds.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor|Amazon SageMaker Pipelines|Amazon CloudWatch|Amazon SageMaker Clarify|AWS Lambda|Amazon EventBridge|Amazon SageMaker Experiments
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor|Amazon CloudWatch|Amazon EventBridge|Amazon SageMaker Pipelines
hint: Model Monitor continuously compares inference data distributions against a baseline captured from the training dataset.
hint2: When drift violations are detected, CloudWatch alarms can trigger automated retraining workflows through event-driven architecture.
explanation: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor continuously captures inference requests and compares feature distributions against the training data baseline, detecting data drift using statistical tests and identifying which specific features have shifted. Violations trigger Amazon CloudWatch alarms with configurable thresholds. Amazon EventBridge captures the alarm events and routes them to Amazon SageMaker Pipelines, which automatically executes the retraining workflow — fetching new data, retraining the model, evaluating performance, and conditionally deploying the updated model.

scenarioDescription: A logistics company needs to forecast demand for 50,000 SKUs across 500 warehouse locations. The time series data includes 3 years of daily sales history with related features like promotions, holidays, and weather. They need probabilistic forecasts (P10, P50, P90) to optimize inventory levels.
toolboxServices: Amazon Forecast|Amazon SageMaker DeepAR|Amazon SageMaker Built-in Algorithms|Amazon Timestream|Amazon QuickSight|AWS Glue|Amazon Lookout for Metrics
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker DeepAR|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: SageMaker's DeepAR algorithm is specifically designed for training a single model across many related time series simultaneously.
hint2: DeepAR produces probabilistic forecasts (quantile predictions) natively, outputting P10, P50, P90 confidence intervals without post-processing.
explanation: Historical sales data with related time series features (promotions, holidays, weather) are stored in Amazon S3 in JSON Lines format. Amazon SageMaker DeepAR trains a single autoregressive RNN model across all 50,000 SKU × 500 location combinations simultaneously, learning cross-series patterns that improve accuracy for low-volume items. DeepAR natively produces probabilistic forecasts — P10, P50, P90 quantiles — deployed via Amazon SageMaker Hosting, enabling the logistics team to optimize inventory with confidence intervals rather than point estimates.

scenarioDescription: A security company needs to build an IP address threat detection system. Given a set of known legitimate IP-to-account mappings, the model must identify when an IP address is used with an account it has never been associated with before, indicating potential account takeover.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker IP Insights|Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon SageMaker Random Cut Forest|Amazon Macie|AWS WAF|Amazon SageMaker KNN|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker IP Insights|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: SageMaker has a purpose-built algorithm that learns statistical associations between IP addresses and entities like account IDs.
hint2: IP Insights uses neural network embeddings to capture IP-entity relationships and scores new pairs by how anomalous they are.
explanation: Historical IP-to-account mapping data is stored in Amazon S3 as CSV with (entity, IP) pairs. Amazon SageMaker IP Insights trains a neural network that learns embeddings for both IP addresses and account entities, capturing the statistical patterns of legitimate associations. The model is deployed to Amazon SageMaker Hosting — at inference time, it scores new (account, IP) pairs and returns a compatibility score, where low scores indicate the IP has never been associated with that account, flagging potential account takeover attempts.

scenarioDescription: A data science team needs to handle a highly imbalanced dataset for fraud detection where only 0.1% of transactions are fraudulent. They need to apply appropriate sampling techniques during training and select evaluation metrics that are meaningful for rare-event classification.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Processing|Amazon SageMaker XGBoost|Amazon SageMaker Autopilot|SMOTE|Amazon SageMaker Clarify|Amazon SageMaker Experiments|AWS Glue
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon SageMaker Processing|Amazon SageMaker XGBoost|Amazon SageMaker Experiments
hint: SageMaker Processing can run custom SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) scripts to balance the training dataset.
hint2: For imbalanced classification, use AUC-PR (Precision-Recall) and F1 score instead of accuracy — XGBoost's scale_pos_weight parameter also helps.
explanation: Amazon SageMaker Processing runs a custom preprocessing job that applies SMOTE to synthetically oversample the 0.1% fraud class, creating balanced training data without losing majority class information. Amazon SageMaker XGBoost trains the fraud classifier with the scale_pos_weight hyperparameter set to the class imbalance ratio (1000:1) and optimizes for AUC rather than accuracy, since accuracy is misleading at 99.9% class imbalance. Amazon SageMaker Experiments tracks multiple runs with different sampling strategies and evaluation metrics (AUC-PR, F1, recall) to identify the optimal approach.

scenarioDescription: A research team needs to find the optimal hyperparameters for a deep learning model with 15 hyperparameters. Grid search is computationally infeasible, and they want an intelligent search strategy that learns from previous evaluations to converge on the best configuration faster than random search.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning|Amazon SageMaker Experiments|Amazon SageMaker Autopilot|AWS Step Functions|Amazon SageMaker Training|Amazon EC2|AWS Batch
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning|Amazon SageMaker Training|Amazon SageMaker Experiments
hint: SageMaker's Automatic Model Tuning (HPO) uses Bayesian optimization by default, which builds a probabilistic model of the objective function.
hint2: Bayesian optimization intelligently selects the next hyperparameter combination based on an acquisition function that balances exploration and exploitation.
explanation: Amazon SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning (Hyperparameter Optimization) uses Bayesian optimization to intelligently search the 15-dimensional hyperparameter space — it builds a Gaussian Process surrogate model of the objective function and uses an acquisition function to select promising configurations, converging faster than random search. Each configuration launches an Amazon SageMaker Training job that trains and evaluates the model. Amazon SageMaker Experiments logs all tuning trials with their hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts for comparison and reproducibility.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to deploy a model that receives unpredictable traffic — zero requests for hours, then sudden bursts of 10,000 requests per minute. They want to minimize costs during idle periods (paying nothing when there's no traffic) while still meeting a 1-second latency SLA during bursts.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference|Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference|Amazon SageMaker Async Inference|Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform|AWS Lambda|Amazon API Gateway|Amazon SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference
hint: A serverless inference option scales to zero when idle and automatically provisions compute when requests arrive.
hint2: Serverless Inference has a configurable memory size and max concurrency — but be aware of cold start latency for the first request after idle.
explanation: Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference is the optimal choice for this unpredictable traffic pattern — it scales to zero during idle periods (zero cost) and automatically provisions compute capacity when requests arrive, scaling up to handle burst traffic. The configurable MemorySizeInMB and MaxConcurrency settings allow tuning for the 10,000 requests/minute burst requirement. While cold starts add latency for the first request after idle, subsequent requests within the same burst meet the 1-second SLA, making it the most cost-effective option compared to always-on real-time endpoints.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to label 100,000 images for a computer vision project. They want to use a combination of human annotators and automated ML-assisted labeling to reduce cost and time. Ambiguous images where the ML model is uncertain should be routed to human reviewers.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth|Amazon Mechanical Turk|Amazon Rekognition|Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)|AWS Lambda|Amazon SageMaker Autopilot|Amazon Textract
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth|Amazon Augmented AI (A2I)
hint: Ground Truth uses active learning to automatically label high-confidence examples and only sends uncertain ones to human annotators.
hint2: A2I creates human review workflows that can be triggered when ML confidence scores fall below configurable thresholds.
explanation: Unlabeled images are stored in Amazon S3. Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth manages the labeling workflow using active learning — it trains an intermediate ML model that automatically labels high-confidence images and routes only uncertain examples to human annotators (from private teams, MTurk, or vendor workforces), reducing labeling costs by up to 70%. Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) adds a human review loop for the most ambiguous predictions, creating structured review workflows where human reviewers validate or correct ML-generated labels based on configurable confidence thresholds.

scenarioDescription: A data science team needs to discover hidden topic structures in a corpus of 2 million news articles. They want an unsupervised approach that identifies the main topics, assigns topic proportions to each document, and can process new documents at inference time to determine their topic composition.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker NTM (Neural Topic Model)|Amazon SageMaker LDA|Amazon Comprehend|Amazon SageMaker BlazingText|Amazon Kendra|Amazon SageMaker Seq2Seq|Amazon SageMaker Object2Vec
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker NTM (Neural Topic Model)|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: SageMaker offers two built-in topic modeling algorithms — NTM uses neural variational inference and typically produces more coherent topics than the classical approach.
hint2: NTM takes a bag-of-words representation as input and outputs a topic distribution vector for each document.
explanation: The news article corpus is stored in Amazon S3 as bag-of-words vectors (term-frequency or TF-IDF representations). Amazon SageMaker NTM (Neural Topic Model) uses a neural variational autoencoder to discover latent topic structures unsupervised — it learns topic-word distributions and assigns topic proportion vectors to each document, typically producing more coherent and interpretable topics than classical LDA. The trained model is deployed to Amazon SageMaker Hosting to process new articles at inference time, returning their topic composition vectors for downstream classification or recommendation.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to deploy 200 different ML models for 200 different customer tenants. Each model is small (under 500 MB) and receives low individual traffic, but the aggregate traffic across all tenants is significant. They need to minimize infrastructure costs while maintaining low latency.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints|Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference|Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference|AWS Lambda|Amazon ECS|Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform|Amazon SageMaker Inference Pipelines
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints
hint: A specialized endpoint type can host hundreds of models behind a single endpoint, dynamically loading and unloading models based on traffic patterns.
hint2: Multi-Model Endpoints share the same compute infrastructure across all models, with an LRU cache that keeps frequently accessed models in memory.
explanation: All 200 tenant model artifacts are stored in Amazon S3. Amazon SageMaker Multi-Model Endpoints host all 200 models behind a single endpoint and shared compute fleet — models are dynamically loaded into memory on first request and cached using an LRU (Least Recently Used) strategy. This dramatically reduces costs compared to 200 individual endpoints, since the shared infrastructure handles the aggregate traffic while individual low-traffic models are loaded/unloaded as needed, maintaining low latency for active tenants.

scenarioDescription: A data science team needs to build an end-to-end ML pipeline that automatically retrains weekly, evaluates model quality against a threshold, and conditionally deploys only if the new model outperforms the current production model. The pipeline must be version-controlled and reproducible.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Pipelines|AWS Step Functions|Amazon SageMaker Model Registry|Amazon EventBridge|AWS CodePipeline|Amazon SageMaker Experiments|AWS Lambda
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon EventBridge|Amazon SageMaker Pipelines|Amazon SageMaker Model Registry|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: SageMaker Pipelines is purpose-built for ML workflows with native steps for processing, training, evaluation, and conditional deployment.
hint2: The Model Registry stores model versions with approval status, enabling conditional promotion from 'PendingApproval' to 'Approved' based on evaluation metrics.
explanation: Amazon EventBridge triggers the weekly pipeline execution on a cron schedule. Amazon SageMaker Pipelines orchestrates the end-to-end ML workflow — processing, training, evaluation, and a ConditionStep that compares the new model's metrics against the production baseline threshold. If the new model outperforms, it's registered in Amazon SageMaker Model Registry with version metadata and approval status. Upon approval, the model is deployed to Amazon SageMaker Hosting, completing the automated CI/CD for ML with full lineage tracking and reproducibility.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to perform inference on 50 million records stored in S3 for a monthly batch scoring job. The job runs once per month, takes approximately 4 hours, and does not require real-time responses. They want to minimize costs and avoid maintaining always-on infrastructure.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform|Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference|Amazon SageMaker Async Inference|Amazon SageMaker Processing|AWS Glue|Amazon EMR|AWS Lambda
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform|Amazon S3
hint: Batch Transform provisions compute only for the duration of the job and automatically terminates instances when complete.
hint2: Batch Transform supports data parallelism across multiple instances and can split large input files for distributed processing.
explanation: The 50 million records reside in Amazon S3. Amazon SageMaker Batch Transform is the optimal choice for large-scale offline scoring — it provisions a fleet of instances, distributes the input data across them for parallel inference, and automatically terminates all instances when the job completes. This eliminates the cost of always-on real-time endpoints for a job that runs only once per month. Results are written back to Amazon S3, and Batch Transform supports configurable output formats including joining predictions with input data.

scenarioDescription: A computer vision team needs to train an object detection model to identify defects on a manufacturing assembly line. They have 10,000 labeled images with bounding box annotations. The model must detect multiple defect types simultaneously in each image and return bounding box coordinates with confidence scores.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker Object Detection|Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels|Amazon SageMaker Image Classification|Amazon Lookout for Vision|AWS Panorama|Amazon SageMaker Semantic Segmentation|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker Object Detection|Amazon SageMaker Hosting
hint: SageMaker's built-in Object Detection algorithm supports both SSD and Faster R-CNN architectures with transfer learning from pre-trained models.
hint2: Object Detection returns bounding box coordinates (x, y, width, height), class labels, and confidence scores for every detected object in the image.
explanation: Labeled images with bounding box annotations are stored in Amazon S3 in RecordIO or JSON manifest format. Amazon SageMaker Object Detection trains a model using either SSD (Single Shot Detector) or Faster R-CNN architecture with transfer learning from pre-trained ImageNet models, learning to detect and localize multiple defect types simultaneously within each image. The trained model is deployed to Amazon SageMaker Hosting for real-time inference, returning bounding box coordinates, defect class labels, and confidence scores for each detected defect on the assembly line.

scenarioDescription: A data science team needs to reduce the dimensionality of a 10,000-feature genomics dataset to 50 principal components for visualization and downstream clustering. The dataset has 1 million samples and doesn't fit in memory on a single machine. They need a distributed approach.
toolboxServices: Amazon SageMaker PCA|Amazon SageMaker Built-in Algorithms|Amazon EMR|scikit-learn|Amazon SageMaker Processing|Amazon SageMaker Random Cut Forest|Amazon SageMaker KNN
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon S3|Amazon SageMaker PCA|Amazon SageMaker KNN
hint: SageMaker's built-in PCA algorithm supports distributed training across multiple instances for datasets that exceed single-machine memory.
hint2: After dimensionality reduction, the 50-component representations can be clustered using SageMaker's KNN algorithm in unsupervised mode.
explanation: The high-dimensional genomics dataset is stored in Amazon S3. Amazon SageMaker PCA performs distributed Principal Component Analysis across multiple instances, reducing the 10,000 features to 50 principal components — SageMaker's implementation uses an approximate algorithm that scales to datasets too large for single-machine memory. The reduced 50-dimensional representations are then fed to Amazon SageMaker KNN for clustering, grouping similar genomic profiles together in the lower-dimensional space for biological interpretation.
